# Instances

```swift
class Dog {
   var name = ""
   func bark() {
        print("Woof!")
    }
}

```

`Dog` is a Type - it is instructions on what Dog is; what it can do; but it is not a dog.


```swift
let fido = Dog() // instantiating `Dog`
let fluffy = Dog() // instantiating `Dog`
```

- `fido` is an **instance** of `Dog`.
- `fluffy` is an **instance** of `Dog`.


### Instance Messages

- `fido.bark()` is sending a message to the instance `fido` to `bark()`.
- there must be an instance to send messages to.
-  we can send messages to instances to change their properties
- `print(fido.name)` we can send messages to instances to get their properties


```swift
fido.name = "Fido" // sending a message to instance `fido` to change its `name` property

print(fido.name) // "Fido"
print(fluffy.name) // ""  

// fido and fluffy have their own properties - they are distinct instances 
```

### self

- .self allows instances to send messages to themselves 


```swift
class Dog {
   var name = ""
   var whatDoesTheDogSay = "Woof!"

   func bark() {
        print(self.whatDoesTheDogSay)
    }

   func speak() {
    print(self.name + " says " + self.whatDoesTheDogSay) // self refers to the instance
    self.bark()
   }
}

```

```swift
let lassie = Dog()
lassie.name = "Lassie"
lassie.whatDoesTheDogSay = "Woof Woof"
lassie.speak() // "Lassie says Woof Woof Woof Woof"
```


- self refers to this exact instance - the currently running code. 
- self is optional when writing code - the compiler knows which instance you are referring to - it is good to be explicit 

#### private 

- use when you dont want an instance to have its properties changed from the outside

```swift
class Dog {
   private var name = ""
   private var whatDoesTheDogSay = "Woof!"
}

class Cat {
    func changeWhatDogSays(dog: Dog) {
        let annie = Dog()
        annie.whatDoesTheDogSay = "Meow!" // ❌ error
}
```
